🟡 Sinal do Vale

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Sinal do Vale runs a bioregional hub in Brazil's Atlantic Rainforest, serving as a catalyst for regeneration through community-based entrepreneurship and systems integration.

Bioregional Context

Guanabara Bay is one of Brasil's largest and most iconic coastal ecosystems, framed by the Atlantic Forest and surrounded by one of the world's most densely populated urban areas. Once rich in biodiversity and cultural significance, the bay now faces severe pollution from untreated sewage, industrial waste, and urban runoff.

Place
Guanabara Bay, Rua do Catimbau, Paquetá, Rio de Janeiro - RJ, 20397-210, Brazil
links
https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc

Tags

🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🏞️ Demonstrator_Site ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 📷 Visitor_Experiences 🔀 Grants_Programs 🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 💧 Watershed_Repair 🧳 Immersive_Journeys 💲 Structuring_and_Capitalization

Background


Guanabara Bay is one of Brasil's largest and most iconic coastal ecosystems, framed by the Atlantic Forest and surrounded by one of the world's most densely populated urban areas. Once rich in biodiversity and cultural significance, the bay now faces severe pollution from untreated sewage, industrial waste, and urban runoff.

For over 15 years, Sinal do Vale has earned the trust and reputation in this bioregion—situated in the buffer zone between Rio de Janeiro and the Mata Atlântica rainforest. The beautiful Mata Atlântica has incredible biodiversity, but has already lost 88% of its size to date. This bioregion delivers the water, food, and other ecosystem services for Rio and surrounding areas, yet it faces urban encroachment, loss of rivers, and low economic activity leading to poverty.

Origins and Network


Against that backdrop, Sinal do Vale set out years ago to show how a biohub can act as a catalyst inside a bioregion and help grow a bioeconomy.

After 15 years of strong results on very limited budgets, the organization is now working toward self-sufficiency so it can sustain and expand that work for the bioregion.

Activities

🔸

Biocultural Hub

 🔸

Madre Frutas Social Enterprise

 🔸

Camino do Guaraú Eco-Tourism Trail

 🔸

Val Estrela Historic and Cultural Ecotourism Site

 🔸

Terra Umida Agroforestry Corridor

 🔸

SINAL Flow Fund

Vision and Legacy


What Sinal do Vale demonstrates is how a biohub can be a catalyst that creates the conditions for regeneration to flourish across an entire bioregion. For over 15 years, working with very limited financial resources yet achieving amazing results, they've shown what's possible when you anchor action in place, build trust with communities, and weave connections across scales from individual projects to regional bioeconomy.

From the women turning jackfruit into livelihoods at Madre Frutas to the quilombola communities restoring Val Estrela, from the landless farmers regenerating Terra Umida through agroforestry to the protection of golden lion tamarins in the Mata Atlântica—Sinal do Vale is showing how supply chains of value can regenerate landscapes that are so important for culture, for people, and for the web of life that connects us all.
